label: AWS Aurora MySQL
description: Connect to an Amazon Aurora MySQL-compatible cluster using its writer or reader endpoint.

form.title: Connect AWS Aurora MySQL
form.subtitle: Enter your Aurora cluster endpoint, credentials, and connection options.

methods.label: Cluster Endpoint (Direct Connection)
methods.description: Paste your Aurora cluster writer or reader endpoint, port, database name, and credentials. The simplest and most common path for connecting Aurora MySQL to Anton.
methods.how_to:
## Connect via Aurora Cluster Endpoint

Aurora exposes separate endpoints for writes (writer) and reads (reader). Grab the right one from the RDS Console.

### Steps

1. Open the [AWS RDS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/rds/) and navigate to **Databases**.
2. Click your Aurora MySQL cluster (not a specific instance — the cluster row).
3. Under the **Connectivity & security** tab, find:
   - **Writer endpoint** — use this for read/write access (e.g. `mycluster.cluster-xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com`).
   - **Reader endpoint** — use this for read-only, load-balanced replicas (e.g. `mycluster.cluster-ro-xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com`).
4. Note the **Port** (default: `3306`).
5. Enter the **Database name** you want Anton to connect to.
6. Provide the **Username** and **Password** for a database user with appropriate privileges.
7. If your cluster enforces TLS (recommended), toggle **Require SSL** on.

### Gotchas

- Make sure the security group attached to the cluster allows inbound TCP on the chosen port from the machine running Anton.
- If connecting across VPCs or from outside AWS, you may need a public endpoint or a bastion/VPN tunnel.
- Aurora Serverless v1 clusters don't expose persistent TCP endpoints — use the Data API method instead.
- The writer endpoint always routes to the current primary; failovers are transparent within ~30 seconds.

fields.name: endpoint_type
fields.label: Endpoint Type
fields.description: Writer endpoint supports reads and writes; reader endpoint is load-balanced and read-only.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Cluster Endpoint Host
fields.description: Copy the writer or reader endpoint hostname from RDS Console → your cluster → Connectivity & security.

fields.name: port
fields.label: Port
fields.description: Default Aurora MySQL port is 3306. Change only if your cluster uses a custom port.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database Name
fields.description: The name of the specific database (schema) inside the cluster that Anton should connect to.

fields.name: user
fields.label: Username
fields.description: Database user with the necessary privileges. Avoid using the master user in production.

fields.name: password
fields.label: Password
fields.description: Password for the database user above. Stored encrypted and never transmitted to Anton's servers.

fields.name: ssl_enabled
fields.label: Require SSL
fields.description: Strongly recommended. Aurora supports TLS by default; enable this unless your cluster explicitly disables it.

fields.name: ssl_ca
fields.label: SSL CA Certificate (optional)
fields.description: Paste the PEM-encoded AWS RDS CA certificate if you need to verify the server identity. Download the bundle from https://truststore.pki.rds.amazonaws.com/global/global-bundle.pem.

methods.label: IAM Database Authentication
methods.description: Use AWS IAM credentials instead of a static password. Aurora generates a short-lived authentication token via the AWS SDK — no long-lived DB password required.
methods.how_to:
## IAM Database Authentication

Aurora MySQL supports password-free login using short-lived tokens generated by AWS IAM. This requires IAM auth to be enabled on the cluster and an IAM policy granting `rds-db:connect`.

### Prerequisites

- Aurora MySQL 5.7 or 8.0 cluster with **IAM database authentication** enabled (RDS Console → your cluster → Modify → check **IAM database authentication**).
- An IAM user or role with a policy allowing `rds-db:connect` on the cluster's DB resource ARN.

### Steps

1. In the RDS Console, enable **IAM database authentication** on your cluster (Modify → Additional configuration).
2. In IAM, attach a policy to your user/role:
   ```
   { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "rds-db:connect",
     "Resource": "arn:aws:rds-db:REGION:ACCOUNT:dbuser:CLUSTER_RESOURCE_ID/DB_USER" }
   ```
3. Create a database user mapped to IAM: `CREATE USER 'myiamuser' IDENTIFIED WITH AWSAuthenticationPlugin AS 'RDS';`
4. Enter your cluster endpoint, port, and database below.
5. Provide your **AWS Access Key ID** and **Secret Access Key** (or leave blank to use the instance/container's role credentials if Anton runs inside AWS).
6. Anton will generate a 15-minute authentication token automatically before each connection.

### Gotchas

- The IAM token is valid for only 15 minutes. Anton refreshes it on each reconnect automatically.
- SSL is mandatory for IAM auth — the SSL toggle is forced on.
- If Anton runs on an EC2/ECS/Lambda with an instance role, you can leave the key fields empty.

fields.name: endpoint_type
fields.label: Endpoint Type
fields.description: Writer endpoint supports reads and writes; reader endpoint is load-balanced and read-only.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Cluster Endpoint Host
fields.description: Writer or reader endpoint hostname from RDS Console → your cluster → Connectivity & security.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database Name
fields.description: The target database (schema) inside the cluster.

fields.name: db_username
fields.label: Database Username
fields.description: The MySQL user created with AWSAuthenticationPlugin. Must match the IAM policy resource ARN.

fields.name: aws_region
fields.label: AWS Region
fields.description: The AWS region where your Aurora cluster lives (e.g. us-east-1, eu-west-2).

fields.name: aws_access_key_id
fields.label: AWS Access Key ID (optional)
fields.description: Leave blank if Anton runs on an AWS resource with an attached IAM role. Otherwise, provide the key ID.

fields.name: aws_secret_access_key
fields.label: AWS Secret Access Key (optional)
fields.description: Paired with the Access Key ID. Leave blank when using instance/container role credentials.
